title: Nomewaimea infaustana Austin & Rubinoff 2026
author: Austin, Kyhl A.
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-zero
description: Nomewaimea infaustana  (Walsingham, 1907), comb. nov.   Figs 4A, B; 5A, 6A   Epagoge infaustana  Walsingham, 1907: 709; pl. 12., fig. 7.   Capua infaustana  (Walsingham, 1907): Meyrick, 1913b: 13.   Spheterista infaustana  (Walsingham, 1907): Zimmerman, 1978: 449; figs. 229, 238, 248, 257–258.   Diagnosis.   Nomewaimea infaustana  is most similar to  Nomewaimea urerana  (Swezey). At present, we can find no consistent morphological characters to separate them from each other (but see Comments below). The terminal plate of  N .  infaustana  may be slightly larger and the valvae less apically extended than in  N .  urerana , but these characters may be somewhat variable.   Type material.   Epagoge infaustana  Walsingham, 1907.  Lectotype  [here designated], ♂.  Kauaʻi:  M[oun]t[ain]s; 3000–4000 ft [914–1219 m]; vi 1894; Perkins / BM genitalia slide 1914 (NHMUK) [examined].  Paralectotypes  [here designated] (7♂♂, 19♀♀).  Kauaʻi:  2♀♀, 3000–4000 ft [914–1219 m]; v 1894; Perkins (BPBM). 4♀♀, same as previous / BM slide 1915 (NHMUK). 2♀♀, 3000–4000 ft [914–1219 m]; vi 1894; Perkins (BPBM). 2♀♀, same as previous / BM slide 14314 (NHMUK). 1♀, Kahōluamanu; 4000 ft [1219 m]; iv 1895; Perkins (BPBM). 1♀, same as previous (NHMUK).  Molokai:  1♂, above 3000 ft [914 m]; 24 vi 1893; Perkins (NHMUK).  Maui:  1♀, Haleakalā; 5000 ft [1524 m]; v 1896; Perkins (NHMUK).  Hawaiʻi:  1♀, Mt. Kīlauea; vii 1895; Perkins (NHMUK). 1♀, Mt. Kīlauea; viii 1896 (BPBM). 1♂, 1♀, same as previous (NHMUK). 1♂, Kona, above 2000 ft [610 m]; ix 1892 (BPBM). 2♂♂, 1♀, same as previous (NHMUK). 1♀, Olaʻa; 2000 ft [610 m]; xi 1896 / genitalia slide 112 (BPBM). 1♂, 1♀, same as previous (NHMUK). 1♂, Olaʻa; vii 1895; genitalia slide 111 (BPBM) [all examined].   Additional material examined  (37♂♂, 40♀♀).  Kauaʻi:  4♀♀, Kahōluamanu; iv 1920; J. A. Kusche (BPBM). 1♀, same as previous (HDOA). 8♂♂, 7♀♀, Kōkeʻe; 10 vii 1937; E. C. Zimmerman (NHMUK). 2♀♀, same as previous except 8 vii 1937 (NHMUK). 5♂♂, Kōkeʻe State Park, Kahuamaʻa Flat; 4000 ft [1219 m]; 21 viii 1973 / K. & E. Sattler (NHMUK). 1♀, same as previous except 24 viii 1973 (NHMUK). 1♀, same as previous except 9 ix 1973 (NHMUK). 2♂♂, same as previous except 17 vii 1937 (NHMUK). 4♂♂, 1♀, Kōkeʻe; 20 viii 1959; J. W. Beardsley; at light (BPBM). 1♂, same as previous except 24 viii 1959; light trap (BPBM). 1♂, 1♀, Kumuwela; 9 vii [19]32; O. H. Swezey / ex. A. Austin  et al. ; UVLED light sheet / DNA extraction KA0857 / KAA diss. #1082 (UHIM). 1♂, Nā Pali-Kona For [est] Res [erve], Alakaʻi Wild [erness] Pres [erve], edge of Waiakōali Bog 1; 22.1294, -159.5775; 1260 m; 8–10 ii 2022; K.A. Austin; UVLED light sheet / DNA extraction KA0442 / KAA diss. #0699 (UHIM). 1♂, 4♀♀, Kōkeʻe State Park, Awaʻawapuhi Trail; 22.1428, -159.6538; 1235 m; 11 vii 2023; K. A. Austin; UVLED light sheet (UHIM). 1♀, Kōkeʻe State Park, Awaʻawapuhi Trail; 22.1468, -159.6605; 1110 m; 21–22 iii 2022; K. A. Austin; [UV] bucket trap / DNA extraction KA0546 / KAA diss. #0780 (UHIM). 1♂, Nā Pali-Kona F[orest] R[eserve], Alakaʻi Wilderness Preserve; Halepaʻakai Camp; 22.0796, -159.5476; 1310 m; 11–14 vi 2024; J. Ueda; hand collecting adult at night / DNA extraction KA1263 / KAA diss. #1282 (UHIM). 1♀, Nā Pali-Kona For [est] Res [erve], Alakaʻi Swamp Tr [ail]; 1234 m; WGS84 4Q 436358 2449215 [22.146890, -159.617191]; 8–9 viii 2007; W. Haines, D. Rubinoff; UV light trap (UHIM). 4♂♂, 2♀♀, Kōkeʻe State Park, Mohihi R [oa]d, field of  Carex  / Sugi pines; 3600 ft [1097 m]; 17 v [20]05; D. Rubinoff  et al.  / DNA extractions KA00037, 00038 / KAA diss. #0316 (♂), #0317 (♀) (UHIM). 3♂♂, 5♀♀, Kōkeʻe S [tate] P[ark], Mohihi R[oa]d, Kauaikinanā St [ream]; 2 vi [20]04; W. Haines; UV light trap (UHIM). 1♂, Nā Pali-Kona F[orest] R[eserve], Flume nr. Ditch Trail; 04Q-0434895-2447551 [22.131803, -159.631311]; 3400 ft [1036 m]; 17 v [20]05; D. Rubinoff  et al.  (UHIM). 1♂, 1♀, Nā Pali-Kona F [orest] R[eserve], Mohihi R[oa]d [at] Waiakōali Stream; 3400 ft [1036 m]; 17 v [20]05; D. Rubinoff  et al.  / KAA diss. #0283 (♀), #0284 (♂) (UHIM).   Comments.  One male syntype in NHMUK bears a violet “ lectotype ” label (this specimen figured in Zimmerman 1978; Figs 238, 248 as the “ allotype ”). The remaining 17 syntypes in NHMUK each bear a light blue “ paralectotype ” label (including one female illustrated by Zimmerman 1978; Fig. 257 as the “ holotype ”). We can find no published record of this lectotype designation and therefore presently designate the male labeled as “ lectotype ” as the lectotype and the remaining 17 specimens in NHMUK and 9 specimens from the type series as paralectotypes. Zimmerman’s designation of a “ holotype ” is not sufficient to meet Article 74.5 of ICZN (1999).  The type series of  Nomewaimea infaustana  examined by Walsingham (1907) includes multiple species of  Nomewaimea . Only those from Kauaʻi represent  Nomewaimea infaustana . The remaining paralectotypes represent undescribed species of  Nomewaimea .  In addition to the material examined above, we examined ca. 60 additional specimens of  N .  infaustana  in NHMUK collected by Klaus and Edith Sattler on Kauaʻi in 1973 and 1982, but were unable to record all these data during a brief visit to NHMUK in 2023.  Though we were unable to observe consistent morphological differences between  N .  infaustana  and  N .  urerana , we choose to maintain them as distinct species based on a 5.3% minimum interspecific genetic divergence of a 1,494 bp fragment of the cytochrome oxidase I (COI) gene (Austin & Rubinoff, unpublished data).   Biology.   Nomewaimea infaustana  has only been reared from an unidentified species of  Pipturus . Its true host breadth is likely much wider, as we have frequently encountered  N .  infaustana  in localities with no  Pipturus  nearby. It may be able to utilize other species of native Urticaceae, similar to that of its Oʻahu relative,  N .  urerana  (Swezey).   Distribution.   Nomewaimea infaustana  is endemic to Kauaʻi, where it has been collected from elevations between ca. 1000–1300 m. It is likely to also occur at lower elevations, especially in mesic and wet valleys where  Pipturus  spp. grow. Very few recent entomological surveys have targeted such habitat due to the rugged and remote terrain of Kauaʻi.   Conservation status.   Nomewaimea infaustana  can likely be considered secure. It has been recorded in many different locations across Kauaʻi in recent years and can be locally common in some places. Determining its full host range will help to better understand the threats  N .  infaustana  faces and may be important in understanding the evolution of the group.
